title: Visualization Code
text: Visualization and simulation code is organized by lecture, with one directory per lecture.

title: Visualization Code
text:
Lecture code directories

Lecture 01 directory
Lecture 01 workspace plot script

title: Visualization Code
text:
Suggested convention

code/lecture-XX-topic/ for lecture-specific materials
code/shared/ for reusable utilities
Include a short README.md in each directory

title: Lecture 16 Notes: Introduction to Robot Learning
text: Lecture 16 slides

title: Lecture 16 Notes: Introduction to Robot Learning
text: Lecture 16 slides

title: Lecture 16 Notes: Introduction to Robot Learning
text:
2 Key ideas

Robot learning aims to improve behavior from data rather than only hand-crafted rules.
Supervised, imitation, and reinforcement learning solve different robotics problems.
Data quality, safety constraints, and sample efficiency drive method choice.

title: Lecture 16 Notes: Introduction to Robot Learning
text:
3 Learning paradigms

Supervised learning: predict labels or state from data.
Imitation learning: clone expert behavior from demonstrations.
Reinforcement learning: optimize policies via reward and interaction.

title: Lecture 16 Notes: Introduction to Robot Learning
text:
4 Review questions

Which robotics tasks fit imitation learning better than RL?
Why is sim-to-real transfer difficult for learned policies?
How do we evaluate safety when deploying learned controllers?

text: Lecture 14 slides

title: Lecture 14 Notes: Perception II - Camera Modeling and Calibration
text: Lecture 14 slides

title: Lecture 14 Notes: Perception II - Camera Modeling and Calibration
text:
2 Key ideas

Camera models convert 3D geometry to 2D image observations.
Calibration identifies intrinsics/extrinsics and lens distortion parameters.
Reprojection error is a practical metric for calibration quality.

title: Lecture 14 Notes: Perception II - Camera Modeling and Calibration
Lecture 14 Notes: Perception II - Camera Modeling and Calibration
text:
4 Review questions

What is the difference between intrinsic and extrinsic parameters?
How does poor calibration affect 3D perception pipelines?
Why are multiple views needed for robust calibration?

title: Lecture 12 Notes: Reactive Control - Potential Fields, Velocity Fields, and Model Predictive Control
text: Lecture 12 slides

title: Lecture 12 Notes: Reactive Control - Potential Fields, Velocity Fields, and Model Predictive Control
text: Lecture 12 slides

title: Lecture 12 Notes: Reactive Control - Potential Fields, Velocity Fields, and Model Predictive Control
text:
2 Key ideas

Reactive control uses local information for fast online responses.
Potential and velocity fields offer interpretable geometric behavior.
MPC handles constraints explicitly by solving repeated finite-horizon optimization.

title: Lecture 12 Notes: Reactive Control - Potential Fields, Velocity Fields, and Model Predictive Control
text:
3 Typical MPC objective
[ {u{0:H-1}} {k=0}^{H} |x_k - x_k{ref}|_Q2 + {k=0}{H-1}|u_k|_R2 ]
subject to system dynamics and safety constraints.

title: Lecture 12 Notes: Reactive Control - Potential Fields, Velocity Fields, and Model Predictive Control
text:
4 Review questions

What failure modes appear in naive potential fields?
Why is MPC computationally heavier than PID?
Which tasks benefit most from constraint-aware control?

title: Lecture 10 Notes: Control I - Computed Torque Control
text: Lecture 10 slides

title: Lecture 10 Notes: Control I - Computed Torque Control
text: Lecture 10 slides

title: Lecture 10 Notes: Control I - Computed Torque Control
text:
2 Key ideas

Computed torque uses robot dynamics for model-based tracking control.
Feedback linearization can simplify nonlinear joint dynamics.
Practical robustness still depends on parameter uncertainty and disturbances.

title: Lecture 10 Notes: Control I - Computed Torque Control
text:
3 Control law sketch
[ = M(q)(_{des} + K_d + K_p e) + C(q,) + g(q) ]
This form combines model-based feedforward and error feedback for trajectory tracking.

title: Lecture 10 Notes: Control I - Computed Torque Control
text:
4 Review questions

Why does computed torque rely on an accurate dynamics model?
What happens when (M, C, g) are estimated poorly?
How can robust or adaptive terms improve stability in practice?

title: Lecture 08 Notes: Robot Simulation - Modern Physics Engines and Contact Dynamics
text: Lecture 08 slides

title: Lecture 08 Notes: Robot Simulation - Modern Physics Engines and Contact Dynamics
text: Lecture 08 slides

title: Lecture 08 Notes: Robot Simulation - Modern Physics Engines and Contact Dynamics
text:
2 Key ideas

Simulation enables fast prototyping and reproducible testing.
Physics engines approximate contact, friction, and constraints numerically.
Stable simulation requires consistent integration step and model parameters.

title: Lecture 08 Notes: Robot Simulation - Modern Physics Engines and Contact Dynamics
text:
3 Practical checklist

Validate inertial and contact parameters.
Use fixed time steps for controller benchmarking.
Compare simulated trajectories against hardware logs.

title: Lecture 08 Notes: Robot Simulation - Modern Physics Engines and Contact Dynamics
text:
4 Review questions

Why do contact-rich tasks diverge more in sim-to-real transfer?
How does step size affect stability and runtime?
What metrics best indicate simulation fidelity for your task?

title: Lecture 06 Notes: Robot Modeling III - Velocity Kinematics and Inverse Kinematics
text: Lecture 06 slides

title: Lecture 06 Notes: Robot Modeling III - Velocity Kinematics and Inverse Kinematics
text: Lecture 06 slides

title: Lecture 06 Notes: Robot Modeling III - Velocity Kinematics and Inverse Kinematics
text:
2 Key ideas

Velocity kinematics links joint velocity to task-space velocity.
The Jacobian captures local motion sensitivity at each configuration.
Inverse kinematics can be solved iteratively using Jacobian-based updates.

title: Lecture 06 Notes: Robot Modeling III - Velocity Kinematics and Inverse Kinematics
text:
3 Core relation
[ = J(q), J(q)^{}_{des} ]

title: Lecture 06 Notes: Robot Modeling III - Velocity Kinematics and Inverse Kinematics
text:
4 Review questions

What does a Jacobian singularity mean physically?
Why is (J^) useful for redundant systems?
How does damping improve numerical IK near singularities?

title: Lecture 02 Notes: Actuation I - Electric Motors, Transmissions, and Torque Generation
text: Lecture 02 slides

title: Lecture 02 Notes: Actuation I - Electric Motors, Transmissions, and Torque Generation
text:
2 Key ideas

Actuators convert electrical power into controllable mechanical motion.
Transmission choices trade output speed, torque, and backdrivability.
Continuous torque and thermal limits often determine usable performance.

title: Lecture 02 Notes: Actuation I - Electric Motors, Transmissions, and Torque Generation
text:
3 Core equations

Motor torque: (_m = K_t i)
Back EMF: (e = K_e _m)
With gear ratio (N): (_{out} N m), ({out} = _m / N)

title: Lecture 02 Notes: Actuation I - Electric Motors, Transmissions, and Torque Generation
text:
4 Review questions

Why does a higher gear ratio increase output torque but reduce speed?
How do efficiency losses change actuator sizing decisions?
Why is thermal behavior critical for repeated robotic tasks?

title: Lecture 08: Actuation II - Actuator Dynamics and Low-Level Control
text:
Learning goals

Model basic actuator dynamics.
Understand cascaded servo control loops.
Evaluate delay, saturation, and bandwidth limits.

title: Lecture 08: Actuation II - Actuator Dynamics and Low-Level Control
text:
Typical low-level architecture

Current loop (fast inner loop)
Velocity loop
Position loop (outer loop)

title: Lecture 08: Actuation II - Actuator Dynamics and Low-Level Control
text:
Practical control issues

Saturation and anti-windup
Quantization and sensor noise
Sample period and communication delay

title: Lecture 08: Actuation II - Actuator Dynamics and Low-Level Control
text:
Summary

Low-level servo quality shapes high-level task performance.
Stable cascaded tuning is essential for reliable robots.

title: Lecture 12: Reactive Control - Potential Fields, Velocity Fields, and Model Predictive Control
text:
Learning goals

Compare reactive control strategies for online response.
Understand potential-field and velocity-field design.
See how MPC handles constraints explicitly.

title: Lecture 12: Reactive Control - Potential Fields, Velocity Fields, and Model Predictive Control
text:
Reactive methods

Potential fields: gradient-based motion
Velocity fields: direct vector guidance
MPC: finite-horizon optimization under constraints

title: Lecture 12: Reactive Control - Potential Fields, Velocity Fields, and Model Predictive Control
text:
MPC template
[ {u{0:H-1}} _{k=0}{H}|x_k-x_k{ref}|Q^2 + {k=0}{H-1}|u_k|_R2 ]
subject to dynamics and safety constraints.

title: Lecture 12: Reactive Control - Potential Fields, Velocity Fields, and Model Predictive Control
text:
Summary

Reactive control is fast and practical.
Constraint handling is key for safe robot deployment.

title: Lecture 10: Control I - Computed Torque Control
text:
Learning goals

Understand model-based control for robot joints.
Derive computed torque control from manipulator dynamics.
Analyze effects of model mismatch on tracking.

title: Lecture 10: Control I - Computed Torque Control
text:
Control law
[ = M(q)(_{des} + K_d + K_p e) + C(q,) + g(q) ]

Dynamics compensation linearizes nominal behavior.
Feedback terms stabilize tracking error.

title: Lecture 10: Control I - Computed Torque Control
text:
Practical concerns

Parameter uncertainty
Unmodeled friction/contact
Saturation and safety constraints

title: Lecture 10: Control I - Computed Torque Control
text:
Summary

Computed torque can yield high-accuracy tracking.
Robustness depends on model quality and gain design.

title: Lecture 06: Robot Simulation - Modern Physics Engines and Contact Dynamics
text:
Learning goals

Understand the simulation stack for robotics.
Compare common physics engines and assumptions.
Analyze contact modeling and numerical stability.

title: Lecture 06: Robot Simulation - Modern Physics Engines and Contact Dynamics
text:
Simulation pipeline

Scene and robot model definition
Physics integration and contact solve
Sensor/actuator loop emulation
Logging and evaluation

title: Lecture 06: Robot Simulation - Modern Physics Engines and Contact Dynamics
text:
Contact dynamics considerations

Time step and solver iterations affect stability.
Friction/contact parameters strongly affect behavior.
Sim-to-real mismatch must be expected and managed.

title: Lecture 06: Robot Simulation - Modern Physics Engines and Contact Dynamics
text:
Summary

Simulation accelerates iteration before hardware tests.
Contact fidelity is critical for manipulation tasks.

title: Lecture 04: Robot Modeling III - Velocity Kinematics and Inverse Kinematics
text:
Learning goals

Relate joint velocities to task-space velocities.
Derive and interpret manipulator Jacobians.
Solve inverse kinematics iteratively.

title: Lecture 04: Robot Modeling III - Velocity Kinematics and Inverse Kinematics
text:
Differential kinematics
[ = J(q) ]

(J(q)): local mapping from joint to task space
Singularities reduce directional controllability

title: Lecture 04: Robot Modeling III - Velocity Kinematics and Inverse Kinematics
text:
Inverse kinematics idea
[ J(q)^_{des} ]

Pseudoinverse enables numerical IK updates.
Damped variants improve behavior near singularity.

title: Lecture 04: Robot Modeling III - Velocity Kinematics and Inverse Kinematics
text:
Summary

Jacobians connect geometry and motion.
IK turns task goals into executable joint commands.

title: Lecture 14: Perception II - Camera Modeling and Calibration
text:
Learning goals

Understand camera geometry for robotics.
Distinguish intrinsic and extrinsic parameters.
Perform and validate camera calibration.

title: Lecture 14: Perception II - Camera Modeling and Calibration
Lecture 14: Perception II - Camera Modeling and Calibration
text:
Calibration workflow

Collect target images from diverse viewpoints
Solve for (K, R, t) and distortion terms
Validate via reprojection error

title: Lecture 14: Perception II - Camera Modeling and Calibration
text:
Summary

Calibration is foundational for 3D perception.
Better calibration yields more reliable downstream control.

title: Lecture 02: Robot Modeling I - Rigid Body Motions
text:
Learning goals

Describe rigid-body motion in 2D and 3D.
Use homogeneous transforms for frame-to-frame mapping.
Understand the role of SE(3) in robotics.

title: Lecture 02: Robot Modeling I - Rigid Body Motions
text:
Coordinate frames and transforms

A frame defines position and orientation.
A transform maps vectors from one frame to another.
Composition order matters in chained transforms.

title: Lecture 02: Robot Modeling I - Rigid Body Motions
text:
Core representation
[ T =
\[\begin{bmatrix}
R & p \\
0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}\]
, R SO(3), ; T SE(3) ]

title: Lecture 02: Robot Modeling I - Rigid Body Motions
text:
Summary

Rigid-body transforms are the geometric language of robotics.
SE(3) is the foundation for kinematics, perception, and control.

title: Introduction to Robotics
text:
Robotics combines mechanics, electronics, and computation to build autonomous physical systems. This course introduces the core ideas behind modeling, simulation, planning, control, and perception.
By the end of this course, you will be able to:

Understand the fundamentals of robot simulation (Left: MuJoCo, Right: IsaacLab)
Video Video
Understand a complete perception-to-action pipeline, providing the foundation to build real-world robotic systems later
Video Video Video Video

title: Lecture 01: Introduction - A Roadmap to Autonomous Robotic Systems
text:
Learning goals

Understand the scope of modern robotics.
Connect modeling, actuation, perception, planning, and control.
See how all course modules fit into one robotics pipeline.

title: Lecture 01: Introduction - A Roadmap to Autonomous Robotic Systems
text:
What is an autonomous robot?
A practical robot system includes:

Mechanical body and actuation
Sensing and state estimation
Decision making (planning and control)
Software/hardware integration in the loop

title: Lecture 01: Introduction - A Roadmap to Autonomous Robotic Systems
text:
Course roadmap

Modeling and simulation foundations
Actuation and low-level dynamics
Planning and control
Perception and learning-based methods

title: Lecture 01: Introduction - A Roadmap to Autonomous Robotic Systems
text:
Summary

Robotics is a systems discipline.
Each lecture builds one layer of a complete autonomy stack.

title: Lecture 13: Perception I - Sensors, Measurements, Noise, and State Estimation
text:
Learning goals

Compare sensing modalities used in robotics.
Model process/measurement uncertainty.
Understand the structure of state-estimation pipelines.

title: Lecture 13: Perception I - Sensors, Measurements, Noise, and State Estimation
text:
Estimation model
[ x_k = f(x_{k-1},u_{k-1}) + w_k, z_k = h(x_k) + v_k ]

(w_k): process noise
(v_k): measurement noise

title: Lecture 13: Perception I - Sensors, Measurements, Noise, and State Estimation
text:
Typical pipeline

Prediction from dynamics
Sensor update from observations
Uncertainty-aware output to planning/control

title: Lecture 13: Perception I - Sensors, Measurements, Noise, and State Estimation
text:
Summary

Perception quality depends on both sensors and inference.
Uncertainty-aware estimation improves robustness.

title: Lecture 03: Robot Modeling II - Forward Kinematics
text:
Learning goals

Map joint variables to end-effector pose.
Build serial-chain transforms from link models.
Interpret robot workspace from kinematic structure.

title: Lecture 03: Robot Modeling II - Forward Kinematics
text:
Forward kinematics
[ x = f(q), T_{0e}(q) = {i=1}^{n} T{i-1,i}(q_i) ]

Input: joint configuration (q)
Output: task-space pose (x)

title: Lecture 03: Robot Modeling II - Forward Kinematics
text:
Practical notes

Link dimensions and joint offsets must be calibrated.
Kinematic errors directly impact planning and control quality.

title: Lecture 03: Robot Modeling II - Forward Kinematics
text:
Summary

Forward kinematics is the first step toward motion generation.
It provides the geometry required for inverse and differential methods.

title: Lecture 05: Robot Modeling IV - Dynamics of Open Chains
text:
Learning goals

Derive open-chain manipulator dynamics.
Interpret inertia, Coriolis, and gravity terms.
Connect dynamic models to simulation and control.

title: Lecture 05: Robot Modeling IV - Dynamics of Open Chains
text:
Standard equation of motion
[ M(q) + C(q,) + g(q) = ]

(M(q)): inertia coupling
(C(q,)): velocity-dependent effects
(g(q)): gravitational load

title: Lecture 05: Robot Modeling IV - Dynamics of Open Chains
text:
Why dynamics matter

Inverse dynamics for feedforward control
Better simulation fidelity
Safer actuator sizing

title: Lecture 05: Robot Modeling IV - Dynamics of Open Chains
text:
Summary

Dynamics tells us how torque becomes motion.
Accurate models improve tracking and robustness.

title: Lecture 09: Planning - Trajectory Generation and Motion Planning
text:
Learning goals

Formulate motion planning in configuration space.
Compare search-based and sampling-based methods.
Distinguish path planning from trajectory generation.

title: Lecture 09: Planning - Trajectory Generation and Motion Planning
text:
Planning workflow

Build geometry and constraints
Find feasible path in (C)-space
Time-parameterize into executable trajectory

title: Lecture 09: Planning - Trajectory Generation and Motion Planning
text:
Common methods

Graph search (A*, Dijkstra variants)
Sampling planners (PRM, RRT family)
Post-processing (shortcutting, smoothing)

title: Lecture 09: Planning - Trajectory Generation and Motion Planning
text:
Summary

Planning bridges goals and physical execution.
Representation and constraints determine planner behavior.

title: Lecture 11: Control II - Operational-Space Control
text:
Learning goals

Control motion directly in task space.
Use Jacobian mappings between force and torque.
Handle redundancy with null-space objectives.

title: Lecture 11: Control II - Operational-Space Control
text:
Task-space relations
[ = J(q), = J(q)^T F ]

End-effector objective is expressed in Cartesian space.
Joint torques are generated through Jacobian transpose mapping.

title: Lecture 11: Control II - Operational-Space Control
text:
Redundancy handling

Primary task: end-effector tracking
Secondary task: posture/joint-limit avoidance
Null-space projection preserves priority

title: Lecture 11: Control II - Operational-Space Control
text:
Summary

Operational-space control aligns control with task goals.
Redundancy can be exploited for safer, smoother behavior.

title: Lecture 07: Actuation I - Electric Motors, Transmissions, and Torque Generation
text:
Learning goals

Explain how electric motors generate torque.
Understand gear/transmission trade-offs.
Connect actuator sizing to robot task demands.

title: Lecture 07: Actuation I - Electric Motors, Transmissions, and Torque Generation
text:
Core actuator relations
[ _m = K_t i, e = K_e _m ]

Torque constant (K_t) links current to torque.
Back-EMF limits high-speed operation.

title: Lecture 07: Actuation I - Electric Motors, Transmissions, and Torque Generation
text:
Transmission effects

Higher gear ratio increases output torque.
Efficiency and backlash impact control quality.
Thermal constraints bound continuous performance.

title: Lecture 07: Actuation I - Electric Motors, Transmissions, and Torque Generation
text:
Summary

Actuation sets physical limits of robot behavior.
Electrical, mechanical, and thermal design must be co-optimized.

title: Lecture 15: A Brief Introduction to Learning-Based Robot Control
text:
Learning goals

Understand why learning is used in robot control.
Compare imitation learning and reinforcement learning.
Identify practical barriers for real-world deployment.

title: Lecture 15: A Brief Introduction to Learning-Based Robot Control
text:
Learning-based control paradigms

Behavior cloning from demonstrations
Reinforcement learning from reward-driven interaction
Hybrid model-based plus learned residual approaches

title: Lecture 15: A Brief Introduction to Learning-Based Robot Control
text:
Deployment considerations

Data quality and coverage
Safety constraints and fallback policies
Sim-to-real transfer and domain randomization

title: Lecture 15: A Brief Introduction to Learning-Based Robot Control
text:
Summary

Learning can reduce manual controller engineering.
Robustness, safety, and sample efficiency remain core challenges.

title: Lecture 01: Introduction - A Roadmap to Autonomous Robotic Systems
text:
Definition 1 (Robot)  A robot is a physically embodied, programmable system that:      1. acquires information from its environment through sensors,      2. computes or plans desired actions, and      3. executes those actions through actuators to achieve specified task objectives.











Definition 2 (Articulated Robot)  An articulated robot is a robot composed of rigid links connected by joints.

title: Lecture 01: Introduction - A Roadmap to Autonomous Robotic Systems
text:
Definition 1 (Robot)  A robot is a physically embodied, programmable system that:      1. acquires information from its environment through sensors,      2. computes or plans desired actions, and      3. executes those actions through actuators to achieve specified task objectives.











Definition 2 (Articulated Robot)  An articulated robot is a robot composed of rigid links connected by joints.

title: Lecture 03 Notes: Actuation II - Actuator Dynamics and Low-Level Control
text: Lecture 03 slides

title: Lecture 03 Notes: Actuation II - Actuator Dynamics and Low-Level Control
text: Lecture 03 slides

title: Lecture 03 Notes: Actuation II - Actuator Dynamics and Low-Level Control
text:
2 Key ideas

Low-level control is usually implemented as cascaded current, velocity, and position loops.
Real actuators have delay, saturation, friction, and finite sampling effects.
Stability margins depend on both plant dynamics and controller timing.

title: Lecture 03 Notes: Actuation II - Actuator Dynamics and Low-Level Control
text:
3 Typical model
A simplified actuator model can be written as: [ J + b = - _{load} ]
This model helps explain why loop bandwidth and damping matter in servo performance.

title: Lecture 03 Notes: Actuation II - Actuator Dynamics and Low-Level Control
text:
4 Review questions

Why are cascaded loops used in motor drives?
How does sampling period affect closed-loop behavior?
What is the practical impact of torque saturation?

title: Lecture 05 Notes: Robot Modeling II - Forward Kinematics

title: Lecture 05 Notes: Robot Modeling II - Forward Kinematics
text:
2 Key ideas

Forward kinematics maps joint configuration (q) to end-effector pose (x).
Kinematic chains are modeled by multiplying link-by-link transforms.
Workspace limits emerge from joint range and link geometry.

title: Lecture 05 Notes: Robot Modeling II - Forward Kinematics
Lecture 05 Notes: Robot Modeling II - Forward Kinematics
text:
4 Review questions

What information is needed to compute forward kinematics?
How does joint offset calibration affect (f(q))?

text: Lecture 07 slides

title: Lecture 07 Notes: Robot Modeling IV - Dynamics of Open Chains

title: Lecture 07 Notes: Robot Modeling IV - Dynamics of Open Chains
text:
2 Key ideas

Dynamics explains how torques produce acceleration and motion.
Open-chain manipulators are commonly modeled with (M, C, g) terms.
Accurate dynamic models improve simulation and model-based control.

title: Lecture 07 Notes: Robot Modeling IV - Dynamics of Open Chains
text:
3 Standard manipulator equation
[ M(q) + C(q,) + g(q) = ]

title: Lecture 07 Notes: Robot Modeling IV - Dynamics of Open Chains
text:
4 Review questions

What role does the mass matrix (M(q)) play?
Why are Coriolis terms coupled with velocity?
When can friction and unmodeled effects dominate predictions?

title: Planning and Control I - Trajectory Generation and Computed Torque Control
text:
A Path is a purely geometric description of the sequence of robot configurations. It can be denoted by \(\theta(s)\) that maps a scalar parameter \(s\), assumed to be \(0\) at the start and \(1\) at the end, to a point in the robot’s configuration space \(\Theta\),
\[
\theta : [0,1] \mapsto \Theta.
\] As \(s\) increases from \(0\) to \(1\), the robot moves along the path.







Example 1 (Sweep of (s) in Configuration Space (Path of 2D Plannar Robot Arm))  Consider a 2D plannar robot arm with two links – where the first link length is \(L_1=1\) and the second link length is \(L_2=0.8\) –, with a configuration \(\theta \in \Theta = [0, 2\pi]^2\), and a path \[
\theta(s)=
\begin{bmatrix}
\theta_1(s)\\
\theta_2(s)
\end{bmatrix}=
\begin{bmatrix}
2 \pi s^2\\
2 \pi s
\end{bmatrix}, \quad s\in[0,1].
\] This path can be visualized as follows.


Show Python code
import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

# Parameter on the line segment [0, 1]
s_grid = np.linspace(0.0, 1.0, 101)

# Arbitrary curve theta(s) = [theta1(s), theta2(s)]^T
theta1 = 2 * np.pi * s_grid**2
theta2 = 2 * np.pi * s_grid

# 2-link planar arm lengths
L1, L2 = 1.0, 0.8

def arm_points(t1, t2):
    x0, y0 = 0.0, 0.0
    x1, y1 = L1 * np.cos(t1), L1 * np.sin(t1)
    x2 = x1 + L2 * np.cos(t1 + t2)
    y2 = y1 + L2 * np.sin(t1 + t2)
    return [x0, x1, x2], [y0, y1, y2]

# Precompute end-effector trajectory
x1_all = L1 * np.cos(theta1)
y1_all = L1 * np.sin(theta1)
x2_all = x1_all + L2 * np.cos(theta1 + theta2)
y2_all = y1_all + L2 * np.sin(theta1 + theta2)

# Initial state
t10, t20 = theta1[0], theta2[0]
x_arm0, y_arm0 = arm_points(t10, t20)

fig = make_subplots(
    rows=1,
    cols=3,
    subplot_titles=(
        "Line segment s in [0, 1]",
        "Curve θ(s) in Θ",
        "2D planar arm",
    ),
    horizontal_spacing=0.10,
)

# (Left) line segment and moving marker
fig.add_trace(
    go.Scatter(x=[0, 1], y=[0, 0], 
    mode="lines", line=dict(color="black", width=3), showlegend=False),
    row=1, col=1,
)
fig.add_trace(
    go.Scatter(x=[s_grid[0]], y=[0], 
    mode="markers", marker=dict(color="crimson", size=11), showlegend=False),
    row=1, col=1,
)

# (Middle) curve in Theta-space and moving point
fig.add_trace(
    go.Scatter(x=theta1, y=theta2, 
    mode="lines", line=dict(color="gray", width=2), showlegend=False),
    row=1, col=2,
)
fig.add_trace(
    go.Scatter(x=[theta1[0]], y=[theta2[0]], 
    mode="markers", marker=dict(color="crimson", size=11), showlegend=False),
    row=1, col=2,
)

# (Right) end-effector path and moving arm
fig.add_trace(
    go.Scatter(
        x=x2_all,
        y=y2_all,
        mode="lines",
        line=dict(color="gray", width=2, dash="dash"),
        name="end-effector path",
        showlegend=False,
    ),
    row=1, col=3,
)
fig.add_trace(
    go.Scatter(
        x=x_arm0,
        y=y_arm0,
        mode="lines+markers",
        line=dict(color="#1f77b4", width=4),
        marker=dict(color=["black", "#1f77b4", "crimson"], size=[8, 9, 10]),
        showlegend=False,
    ),
    row=1, col=3,
)

# Frames for slider animation
frames = []
for i, sv in enumerate(s_grid):
    t1, t2 = theta1[i], theta2[i]
    x_arm, y_arm = arm_points(t1, t2)
    frames.append(
        go.Frame(
            name=f"{sv:.2f}",
            data=[
                go.Scatter(x=[sv], y=[0]),          # left moving marker (trace index 1)
                go.Scatter(x=[t1], y=[t2]),         # middle moving marker (trace index 3)
                go.Scatter(x=x_arm, y=y_arm),       # right arm (trace index 5)
            ],
            traces=[1, 3, 5],
        )
    )
fig.frames = frames

# Slider UI
steps = [
    dict(
        method="animate",
        args=[
            [f"{sv:.2f}"],
            dict(mode="immediate", 
            frame=dict(duration=0, redraw=True), 
            transition=dict(duration=0)),
        ],
        label=f"{sv:.2f}",
    )
    for sv in s_grid
]

fig.update_layout(
    sliders=[
        dict(
            active=0,
            steps=steps,
            x=0.12,
            y=-0.12,
            len=0.78,
            currentvalue=dict(prefix="s = "),
        )
    ],
    margin=dict(l=30, r=30, t=60, b=110),
)

# Axes formatting
fig.update_xaxes(range=[-0.05, 1.05], title_text="s", row=1, col=1, zeroline=False)
fig.update_yaxes(range=[-0.2, 0.2], showticklabels=False, 
row=1, col=1, zeroline=False, scaleratio=1)

fig.update_xaxes(title_text="θ₁", row=1, col=2)
fig.update_yaxes(title_text="θ₂", row=1, col=2, scaleanchor="x2", scaleratio=1)

reach = L1 + L2
fig.update_xaxes(range=[-reach - 0.2, reach + 0.2], row=1, col=3)
fig.update_yaxes(range=[-reach - 0.2, reach + 0.2], row=1, col=3, 
scaleanchor="x3", scaleratio=1)

fig.show()

title: Planning and Control I - Trajectory Generation and Computed Torque Control
text:
A Path is a purely geometric description of the sequence of robot configurations. It can be denoted by \(\theta(s)\) that maps a scalar parameter \(s\), assumed to be \(0\) at the start and \(1\) at the end, to a point in the robot’s configuration space \(\Theta\),
\[
\theta : [0,1] \mapsto \Theta.
\] As \(s\) increases from \(0\) to \(1\), the robot moves along the path.







Example 1 (Sweep of (s) in Configuration Space (Path of 2D Plannar Robot Arm))  Consider a 2D plannar robot arm with two links – where the first link length is \(L_1=1\) and the second link length is \(L_2=0.8\) –, with a configuration \(\theta \in \Theta = [0, 2\pi]^2\), and a path \[
\theta(s)=
\begin{bmatrix}
\theta_1(s)\\
\theta_2(s)
\end{bmatrix}=
\begin{bmatrix}
2 \pi s^2\\
2 \pi s
\end{bmatrix}, \quad s\in[0,1].
\] This path can be visualized as follows.


Show Python code
import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

# Parameter on the line segment [0, 1]
s_grid = np.linspace(0.0, 1.0, 101)

# Arbitrary curve theta(s) = [theta1(s), theta2(s)]^T
theta1 = 2 * np.pi * s_grid**2
theta2 = 2 * np.pi * s_grid

# 2-link planar arm lengths
L1, L2 = 1.0, 0.8

def arm_points(t1, t2):
    x0, y0 = 0.0, 0.0
    x1, y1 = L1 * np.cos(t1), L1 * np.sin(t1)
    x2 = x1 + L2 * np.cos(t1 + t2)
    y2 = y1 + L2 * np.sin(t1 + t2)
    return [x0, x1, x2], [y0, y1, y2]

# Precompute end-effector trajectory
x1_all = L1 * np.cos(theta1)
y1_all = L1 * np.sin(theta1)
x2_all = x1_all + L2 * np.cos(theta1 + theta2)
y2_all = y1_all + L2 * np.sin(theta1 + theta2)

# Initial state
t10, t20 = theta1[0], theta2[0]
x_arm0, y_arm0 = arm_points(t10, t20)

fig = make_subplots(
    rows=1,
    cols=3,
    subplot_titles=(
        "Line segment s in [0, 1]",
        "Curve θ(s) in Θ",
        "2D planar arm",
    ),
    horizontal_spacing=0.10,
)

# (Left) line segment and moving marker
fig.add_trace(
    go.Scatter(x=[0, 1], y=[0, 0], 
    mode="lines", line=dict(color="black", width=3), showlegend=False),
    row=1, col=1,
)
fig.add_trace(
    go.Scatter(x=[s_grid[0]], y=[0], 
    mode="markers", marker=dict(color="crimson", size=11), showlegend=False),
    row=1, col=1,
)

# (Middle) curve in Theta-space and moving point
fig.add_trace(
    go.Scatter(x=theta1, y=theta2, 
    mode="lines", line=dict(color="gray", width=2), showlegend=False),
    row=1, col=2,
)
fig.add_trace(
    go.Scatter(x=[theta1[0]], y=[theta2[0]], 
    mode="markers", marker=dict(color="crimson", size=11), showlegend=False),
    row=1, col=2,
)

# (Right) end-effector path and moving arm
fig.add_trace(
    go.Scatter(
        x=x2_all,
        y=y2_all,
        mode="lines",
        line=dict(color="gray", width=2, dash="dash"),
        name="end-effector path",
        showlegend=False,
    ),
    row=1, col=3,
)
fig.add_trace(
    go.Scatter(
        x=x_arm0,
        y=y_arm0,
        mode="lines+markers",
        line=dict(color="#1f77b4", width=4),
        marker=dict(color=["black", "#1f77b4", "crimson"], size=[8, 9, 10]),
        showlegend=False,
    ),
    row=1, col=3,
)

# Frames for slider animation
frames = []
for i, sv in enumerate(s_grid):
    t1, t2 = theta1[i], theta2[i]
    x_arm, y_arm = arm_points(t1, t2)
    frames.append(
        go.Frame(
            name=f"{sv:.2f}",
            data=[
                go.Scatter(x=[sv], y=[0]),          # left moving marker (trace index 1)
                go.Scatter(x=[t1], y=[t2]),         # middle moving marker (trace index 3)
                go.Scatter(x=x_arm, y=y_arm),       # right arm (trace index 5)
            ],
            traces=[1, 3, 5],
        )
    )
fig.frames = frames

# Slider UI
steps = [
    dict(
        method="animate",
        args=[
            [f"{sv:.2f}"],
            dict(mode="immediate", 
            frame=dict(duration=0, redraw=True), 
            transition=dict(duration=0)),
        ],
        label=f"{sv:.2f}",
    )
    for sv in s_grid
]

fig.update_layout(
    sliders=[
        dict(
            active=0,
            steps=steps,
            x=0.12,
            y=-0.12,
            len=0.78,
            currentvalue=dict(prefix="s = "),
        )
    ],
    margin=dict(l=30, r=30, t=60, b=110),
)

# Axes formatting
fig.update_xaxes(range=[-0.05, 1.05], title_text="s", row=1, col=1, zeroline=False)
fig.update_yaxes(range=[-0.2, 0.2], showticklabels=False, 
row=1, col=1, zeroline=False, scaleratio=1)

fig.update_xaxes(title_text="θ₁", row=1, col=2)
fig.update_yaxes(title_text="θ₂", row=1, col=2, scaleanchor="x2", scaleratio=1)

reach = L1 + L2
fig.update_xaxes(range=[-reach - 0.2, reach + 0.2], row=1, col=3)
fig.update_yaxes(range=[-reach - 0.2, reach + 0.2], row=1, col=3, 
scaleanchor="x3", scaleratio=1)

fig.show()

title: Lecture 11 Notes: Control II - Operational-Space Control
text: Lecture 11 slides

title: Lecture 11 Notes: Control II - Operational-Space Control
text: Lecture 11 slides

title: Lecture 11 Notes: Control II - Operational-Space Control
text:
2 Key ideas

Operational-space control directly regulates end-effector motion/force.
Jacobians map between joint space and task space.
Redundant robots can execute secondary objectives in null space.

title: Lecture 11 Notes: Control II - Operational-Space Control
text:
3 Core relation
[ = J(q), = J^T F + N(q)_{null} ]

title: Lecture 11 Notes: Control II - Operational-Space Control
text:
4 Review questions

Why is task-space control useful for manipulation tasks?
What is null-space motion and when is it beneficial?
How do joint limits change operational-space design?

title: Lecture 13 Notes: Perception I - Sensors, Measurements, Noise, and State Estimation
text: Lecture 13 slides

title: Lecture 13 Notes: Perception I - Sensors, Measurements, Noise, and State Estimation
text: Lecture 13 slides

title: Lecture 13 Notes: Perception I - Sensors, Measurements, Noise, and State Estimation
text:
2 Key ideas

Sensors provide indirect, noisy measurements of robot/environment state.
Estimation combines models and measurements to infer latent state.
Uncertainty tracking is essential for robust decisions.

title: Lecture 13 Notes: Perception I - Sensors, Measurements, Noise, and State Estimation
text:
3 Estimation view
[ x_k = f(x_{k-1}, u_{k-1}) + w_k, z_k = h(x_k) + v_k ]
where (w_k) and (v_k) represent process and measurement noise.

title: Lecture 13 Notes: Perception I - Sensors, Measurements, Noise, and State Estimation
text:
4 Review questions

Why is sensor fusion often better than a single sensor?
How does measurement noise propagate to state uncertainty?
What assumptions are needed for Kalman-style filtering?

title: Lecture 15 Notes: Perception III - Modern Robot Perception
text: Lecture 15 slides

title: Lecture 15 Notes: Perception III - Modern Robot Perception
text: Lecture 15 slides

title: Lecture 15 Notes: Perception III - Modern Robot Perception
text:
2 Key ideas

Modern robot perception combines geometric methods with learned representations.
Multimodal inputs (RGB, depth, language, force) improve task-level understanding.
Robustness to domain shift and uncertainty is essential for deployment.

title: Lecture 15 Notes: Perception III - Modern Robot Perception
text:
3 Common workflow

Build scene representation from multimodal sensor streams.
Run detection/segmentation/tracking with learned or hybrid models.
Estimate uncertainty and hand off structured outputs to planning/control.

title: Lecture 15 Notes: Perception III - Modern Robot Perception
text:
4 Review questions

What benefits does multimodal sensing provide over RGB-only perception?
Why is uncertainty estimation important in robot decision pipelines?
How can domain adaptation reduce performance drop in new environments?
